revision.text:
[[de:Aso (Klan)]][[en:Aso clan]][[fr:Clan Aso]][[it:Clan Aso]][[ja:阿蘇氏]][[ko:아소씨]] {{Short description|Japanese clan|qq=}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|image=Chigai-taka-no-ha.svg|image caption=Mon of the Aso clan|founder=[[Kamuyaimimi]]|parent house={{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}}
{{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}|qq=}} The '''Aso clan''' (阿蘇) is a Japanese clan associated with [[Aso Shrine]]. They descend from [[Kamuyaimimi]].{{Cite book |last1=Takano |first1=Tomoaki |title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine |last2=Uchimura |first2=Hiroaki |publisher=Aso Shrine |year=2006 |location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.|qq=}} The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]]{{Cite book |last=Louis-Frédéric |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&dq=%22Aso+uji%22&pg=PA58 |title=Japan Encyclopedia |date=2002 |publisher=Harvard University Press |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |pages=58 |language=en|qq=}}Matsunaga Naomichi: ''[https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 Kuni no miyatsuko]'', Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved 25 October 2023 but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[Aso Shrine]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]], and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. The Aso family were priests but also rose to power as a powerful samurai clan in the 11th century. == History == {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Richard Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}} divides the history of the Aso clan into three stages{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Aso clan|qq=}} === Origins === The Aso clan originally descended from [[Kamuyaimimi]], a son of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]. It is speculated that many members may be buried in {{ill|Nakadori Kofun Group|ja|中通古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadori Kofun Group|qq=}}.{{cite web | title=Aso Kofun & Shouranzan | website=道の駅 阿蘇 | date=2020-06-24 | url=https://www.aso-denku.jp/english/2020/06/aso-kofun-shouranzan/ | language=ja | access-date=2023-10-25|qq=}} Takashi Tanaka did research on their genealogy.Masanobu Suzuki: ''[https://www.waseda.jp/inst/wias/assets/uploads/2018/09/RB007-017-027.pdf Methodology for Analyzing the Genealogy of Ancient Japanese Clans]'', WIAS Research Bulletin no. 7, 2015 The position of Chief Priest of Aso Shrine is hereditary and is passed down from generation to generation with the oldest male in the family becoming the new head of the shrine. The Aso family has the second oldest recorded lineage in Japan after the Imperial family.{{cite web | first=Zaynab |last=Nakhid |title=Friends of Japan Series: Japanese Shrines | website=Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan | url=https://www.tt.emb-japan.go.jp/itpr_ja/11_000001_00686.html | access-date=2023-10-25 |date=2019-10-09|qq=}} The family themselves are said to be descendants of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto. Their early genealogy frorm {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} is as follows.{{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree|qq=}} {{Tree chart||||||EmJ||EmJ={{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}|qq=}} {{Tree chart|||,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.||qq=}} {{Tree chart||MOM||DAD||DAISY|y|Lee||MOM=Kanakori no Kami|DAD=[[Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto]]|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami|Lee=Hime Miko no Kami|qq=}} {{Tree chart|||!||||!||||,|-|^|-|.||qq=}} {{Tree chart||Koutou||ME|y|SIS||SHIN|y|YAYA|||Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami|qq=}} {{Tree chart|||||||||!||||||,|-|^|-|.||qq=}} {{Tree chart||||||||MOM||||DAD||DAISY|MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto|qq=}} {{Tree chart|||||||||!||||||!||qq=}} {{Tree chart||||MOM|y|DAD||||DAISY|MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family|qq=}} {{Tree chart|||||||!||qq=}} {{Tree chart||||||MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests|qq=}} {{Tree chart/end|qq=}}In recorded history the family started as Imperial representatives called [[Kuni no miyatsuko]]. They looked after worship activities in their area. As they gained power, they became leaders in the Aso district. During this time they were very powerful. === Decline in the Nanboku-chō period === In the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the Aso family faced problems. They strongly supported the [[Southern Court]]. This choice, along with family disagreements, weakened their power. Powerful military families like {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|qid=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu|1=Shimazu clan}} and {{ill|Ōmura clan|en|Ōmura clan|qid=Q385522|lt=Omura|1=Ōmura clan}} also became strong competitors. However, the Aso family always had a leader, known as the Daiguji. But many family members faced difficulties. Many died, others moved away, and some hid in the mountains. === Changes in the Tensho Period === During the Tensho period, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} invaded Satsuma. The Aso family's leader, the Daiguji {{ill|Aso Koremitsu|en|Aso Koremitsu|qid=Q4807470|lt=Aso Koremitsu|1=Aso Koremitsu}}, was a young child then. They were slow in welcoming Hideyoshi and were murdered in 1593 at the age of 12.{{cite book | title=Beiträge zur Japanologie | publisher=Institut für Japanologie an der Universität Wien | issue=Bd. 12 | year=1975 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=n8c4AQAAIAAJ | language=de | access-date=2023-10-25 | page=112|qq=}} But in the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keicho era|1=Keichō}} (1596-1614), things improved for the Aso family. {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|en|Katō Kiyomasa|qid=Q1069843|lt=Kato Kiyomasa|1=Katō Kiyomasa}} got control of the Higo province and helped the Aso family. He brought back old traditions and supported the old {{ill|Shake (social class)|en|Shake (social class)|qid=Q11588367|lt=Shake|1=Shake (social class)}} families. But the Daiguji did not become a territorial magnate again. The Aso family were priests but also rose to power as a powerful samurai clan in the 11th century. They remained in prominence until the 16th century as their feudal lord continued to rule over half of what is now known as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}. This contributed economically to the family and the shrine, allowing them to rebuild shrine buildings and hold festivals. It also made it possible for them to establish branches of the Aso shrine in other locations. Some of those shrines still remain in up to 500 locations to this day. Koreyuki Aso (''Aso Koreyuki in Japanese name order'') was the 91st Chief Priest of Aso Shrine with his son being the 92nd. ==References == {{reflist|qq=}} {{Suwa Faith|qq=}} {{Aso Faith|qq=}} [[en:Aso clan]] [[ja:阿蘇氏]] [[ja:Aso clan]] [[de:Aso (Klan)]] [[fr:Clan Aso]] [[it:Clan Aso]] [[ko:아소씨]] [[de:Aso (Klan)]] [[fr:Clan Aso]] [[it:Clan Aso]] [[ko:아소씨]] {{wikidata link|Q11657653}} [[Category:Clans of Kyūshū Region]] [[Category:Ō clan]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:History of Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Aso clan]]
